Bradfute, Charles H. Calisher, Boris Klempa, Jonas Klingström, Jens H. Kuhn, Lies Laenen, Nicole D. Tischler and Piet Maes The citation for this ICTV Report chapter is the summary published as: Bradfute, S. B., Calisher, C. H., Klempa, B., Klingström, J., Kuhn, J. H., Laenen, L., Tischler, N. D., & Maes, P. (2024). ICTV Virus Taxonomy Profile: Hantaviridae 2024, Journal of General Virology 105, 001975 Corresponding author: Piet Maes ( [email protected] ) Edited by: Holly Hughes and Evelien Adriaenssens Posted: March 2024, updated June 2024 Summary Hantaviridae is a family for negative-sense RNA viruses with genomes of about 10.5–14.6 kb (Table 1 Hantaviridae ). The family includes eight genera ( Actinovirus and Percilovirus (subfamily Actantavirinae ), Agnathovirus (subfamily Agantavirinae ), Loanvirus , Mobatvirus , Orthohantavirus and Thottimvirus (subfamily Mammantavirinae ), and Reptillovirus (subfamily Repantavirinae ). These viruses are maintained in and/or transmitted by small mammals, but also by fish and reptiles. Several orthohantaviruses can infect humans, causing mild, severe, and sometimes-fatal diseases. Hantavirids produce enveloped virions containing three single-stranded RNA segments with open reading frames (ORFs) that encode a nucleoprotein (N), a glycoprotein precursor (GPC), and large (L) protein containing an RNA-directed RNA polymerase (RdRP) domain. Table 1 Hantaviridae. Characteristics of members of the family Hantaviridae. Characteristic Description* Example Hantaan virus (S segment: M14626; M segment: M14627; L segment: X55901), species Orthohantavirus hantanense Virion Enveloped, pleomorphic virions 80–160 nm in diameter with heterodimeric surface spikes Genome Three single-stranded RNA molecules (segments): small (S; 1.0–3.0 kb), medium (M; 3.4–4.8 kb), and large (L; 5.3–6.8 kb) Replication Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes containing anti-genomic RNA serve as coding templates for synthesis of genomic RNA Translation Proteins are produced from capped and non-polyadenylated mRNAs. The 5′-cap structure is obtained via cap-snatching from cellular mRNAs Host range Fish (actinoviruses, agnathoviruses, perciloviruses), mammals (orthohantaviruses), and reptiles (reptilloviruses) Taxonomy Realm Riboviria , kingdom Orthornavirae , phylum Negarnaviricota , class Bunyaviricetes , order Elliovirales ; the family includes four subfamilies, eight genera, and 55 species * mostly based on experiments with mammalian orthohantaviruses Viruses assigned to each of the seven genera form genus-specifc monophyletic clades based on phylogenetic analysis of concatenated L protein/RdRP, glycoprotein and N sequences. Viruses from all seven genera share one or more of the following characteristics: (i) enveloped pleomorphic virions; (ii) tri-segmented, negative-sense RNA genome without polyadenylated tracts at the 3′-end; (iii) genomic sequence complementarity at the 5′- and 3′-ends; and (iv) capped but not polyadenylated virus mRNAs ( Maes et al., 2004 ). Piscine Hosts Genus Actinovirus . Viruses in this genus infect actinopterygian (percid, ogcocephalid, and triacanthodid, and possibly goblid and melanotaeniid) fish. Genus Agnathovirus . Viruses in this genus infect agnathan (myxinid) fish. Genus Percilovirus . Viruses in this genus infect actinopterygian ( gobiid, lophiid ) fish. Mammalian Hosts Genus Loanvirus. Viruses in this genus infect rhinolophid, verspertillionid, and possibly nycterid bats and possibly murid rodents. Genus Mobatvirus. Viruses in this genus infect emballonurid, hipposiderid, pteropodid and possibly molossid, and vespertillionid bats and soricid and talpid eulipotyphla. Genus Orthohantavirus. Viruses assigned to this genus infect soricid and talpid eulipotyphla and muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents. Genus Thottimvirus. Viruses in this genus infect soricid and possibly talpid eulipotyphla. Reptilian Hosts Genus Reptillovirus . Viruses in this genus infect gekkonid and possibly scincid reptiles. Virion Morphology Only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Physicochemical and physical properties Only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Nucleic acid Hantavirids have tri-segmented, negative-sense RNA genomes (S, M, and L segments). The viral mRNAs are not polyadenylated and contain a 5′-methylated cap and several non-templated nucleotides at the 5′-end that are derived from host cell mRNAs via cap snatching ( Schmaljohn et al., 1985 , Jonsson and Schmaljohn 2001 , Vaheri et al., 2013 ). Further information is only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Proteins Hantavirids typically express three structural proteins. The most abundant structural protein in a hantavirion is N, which encapsidates the hantaviral genomic segments. The least abundant protein is L, which mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Two glycoprotein subunits, G N and G C , are produced from GPC and mediate entry of hantavirions ( Schmaljohn et al., 1985 , Spiropoulou 2001 , Vaheri et al., 2013 ). Specifics are only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Lipids Not reported but are likely derived from the Golgi apparatus and/or the host cell plasma membrane and therefore likely composed of phospholipids, glycolipids, fatty acids, and sterols. Carbohydrates Only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Genome organization and replication S RNA encodes N and in some viruses NSs ( Jääskeläinen et al., 2007 , Vera-Otarola et al., 2012 ), M RNA encodes GPC, and the L RNA encodes L with its RdRP, helicase, and endonuclease domains ( Jonsson and Schmaljohn 2001 ) (Figure 1 Hantaviridae ). Specifics are only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Figure 1 Hantaviridae . Genome organization of representative hantaviruses. The 5′- and 3′- ends of all segments are complementary at their termini, likely promoting the formation of panhandle RNP complexes within the virion . GPC , glycoprotein precursor gene; L , large (protein) gene; N , nucleoprotein gene. Biology Hantavirids have been found in actinopterygian (gobiid, lophiid, percid, ogcocephalid, and triacanthodid, and possibly goblid and melanotaeniid) and agnathan (myxinid) fish; in emballonurid, hipposiderid, pteropodid, rhinolophid, verspertillionid, and possibly molossid and nycterid bats; in soricid and talpid eulipotyphla; in muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents; in gekkonid and possibly scincid reptiles; and potentially in coleopteran and plectopteran insects (see genus pages for details). Only orthohantaviruses are known to cause diseases (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Antigenicity Systematic antigenicity studies have only been reported for orthohantaviruses (see Orthohantavirus genus page). England thailandense : after Thailand thottapalayamense : after Thottapalayam , India Thottimvirus : after Thott opalayam virus tigrayense : after ትግራይ ( Tigray Region), Ethiopia triacanthodis : after the fish genus Triacanthod es tulaense : after Тула ( Tula ), Russia xuansonense : after Xuân Sơn , Vietnam wufangense : after after Wùfēng (吾锋), Fújiàn Province (福建省), China Subfamily, genus and species demarcation criteria The availability of at least coding-complete sequences of all three genome segments may be sufficient for hantavirid classification in the absence of a cultured isolate. Demarcation of genera is based upon DivErsity pArtitioning by hieRarchical Clustering (DEmARC) analysis) using concatenated deduced S, M, and L segment expression product sequences ( Hantaviridae: current classification and future perspectives, Viruses, 11, 9, e0788">Laenen et al., 2019 ). DEmARC analysis gave a frequency distribution of Pairwise Evolutionary Distance (PED) values of which the threshold of 0.1 gave an optimal clustering cost of zero and is used as the hantavirid species demarcation criterium. Genera are demarked by a PED-value threshold of 0.95 and subfamilies are demarcated based on their distinct clustering in a Bayesian maximum clade credibility tree and a PED-value threshold of 3.5. Relationships within the family Phylogenetic relationships across the family have been estimated using Bayesian maximum clade credibility trees generated from complete N, GPC, and L amino-acid sequences; similar topologies are obtained with concatenated N and GPC sequences (Figure 2 Hantaviridae ). Figure 2 Hantaviridae . Maximum likelihood phylogenetic tree inferred using IQ-TREE. The tree was constructed based on 53 concatenated S and M segment (amino acid) sequences using the LG+R9 model of sequence evolution. Branch lengths represent the estimated number of substitutions per site. Bootstrap support values (>70%) are indicated at nodes, reflecting the degree of confidence in the branching pattern. Relationships with other taxa Hantavirids are closely related to bunyaviral crulivirids, fimovirids, peribunyavirids, phasmavirids, tulasvirids, and tospovirids ( Wolf et al., 2018 , Herath et al., 2020 ). Related, unclassified viruses Virus name Accession number Virus abbreviation Reference coleopteran hanta-related virus OKIAV221 L: MT153497 ( Käfer et al., 2019 ) plecopteran hanta-related virus OKIAV215 L: MT153434 ( Käfer et al., 2019 ) Virus names and virus abbreviations are not official ICTV designations. Additional unclassified hantavirids that are probable members of established genera are listed under individual genus descriptions. Using the Master Species List Using the Virus Metadata Resource Using the Find the Species tool Using the ICTV Report Webinar Webinar on Virus Taxonomy Webinar on Finding Information GitHub Repository Zenodo Repository Email ICTV Help ‏‏‎ ‎ ICTV Report Book: Hantaviridae Family: Hantaviridae Subfamily: Actantavirinae Genus: Actinovirus Genus: Percilovirus Subfamily: Agantavirinae Genus: Agnathovirus Subfamily: Mammantavirinae Genus: Loanvirus Genus: Mobatvirus Genus: Orthohantavirus Genus: Thottimvirus Subfamily: Repantavirinae Genus: Reptillovirus Authors: Hantaviridae Citation: Hantaviridae References: Hantaviridae Resources: Hantaviridae Species List: Hantaviridae Subfamily: Mammantavirinae Genus: Orthohantavirus Distinguishing features Sixty different viruses are classified in the genus Orthohantavius. These viruses typically infect specific soricid and talpid eulipotyphla and muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents. Orthohantaviruses are the only hantavirids known to cause disease in humans ( Schmaljohn and Hjelle 1997 , Jonsson et al., 2010 , Kuhn and Schmaljohn 2023 , Vial et al., 2023 ). Virion Morphology Orthohantavirions are pleomorphic in shape, mostly spherical of 80–160 nm in diameter ( Huiskonen et al., 2010 ), and surrounded by a membrane envelope that is decorated with glycoprotein (GP) spikes composed of G N and G C subunits (Figure 1 Orthohantavirus ). The virion envelope is 5 nm thick and is decorated with square shaped spikes, consisting of tetrameric G N -G C heterodimers, which protrude 10 nM from the membrane forming a surface lattice of local symmetry interrupted by empty membrane spots ( Hung et al., 1983 , Huiskonen et al., 2010 , Battisti et al., 2011 , Parvate et al., 2019 , Serris et al., 2020 , Guardado-Calvo and Rey 2021 ). Isolated ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes are composed of individual segment genomic RNA encapsidated by nucleoprotein (N) ( Tao et al., 1987 , Huiskonen et al., 2010 , Battisti et al., 2011 , Hepojoki et al., 2012 , Guo et al., 2016 , Arragain et al., 2019 ). Virions assemble and bud from the Golgi complex or cell surface membranes, depending on the virus ( Goldsmith et al., 1995 , Parvate et al., 2019 ). Figure 1 Orthohantavirus. A, B) Electron micrographs of Tula virus particles adapted from ( Huiskonen et al., 2010 ). C) Schematic illustration of an orthohantavirus particle. Shown is a spherical and enveloped (grey) particle with glycoprotein spikes (G N yellow, G C blue) inserted in a bilaminar lipid envelope. The S (small), M (medium), and L (large) RNP (ribonucleoprotein) complexes inside the particle consist of N (nucleoprotein, green) and L (large protein, pink). ViralZone image reproduced courtesy of SwissBioPics. Physicochemical and physical properties Virion buoyant densities in sucrose and CsCl are 1.15–1.18 ( White et al., 1982 ) and 1.20–1.21 g cm −3 , respectively ( Schmaljohn et al., 1983 ). Virions are sensitive to chemical inactivation with, for instance, methanol, paraformaldehyde, acetone/methanol, N -ethylmaleimide or detergents, as well as physical methods such as heat, gamma irradiation or UV irradiation ( Kraus et al., 2005 , Strandin et al., 2011 , Elveborg et al., 2022 ). Nucleic acid Orthohantavirus genomes are linear tri-segmented RNA molecules of negative polarity. The genomes are about 11.1–12.3 kb in length (small [S] segment: 1.2–2.1 kb; medium [M] segment: 3.4–3.8 kb; large [L] segment: 6.5–6.6 kb). Proteins Orthohantaviruses typically express three structural proteins (Table 1 Orthohantavirus ). The most abundant structural protein in an orthohantavirion is N, which encapsidates the orthohantaviral genomic segments. The least abundant protein is L, which mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Two glycoprotein subunits, G N and G C , mediate virion entry. G N and G C and non-structural proteins are encoded as a precursor polyprotein. Table 1 Orthohantavirus . Location and function of orthohantavirus structural proteins. Protein Location, mass, and function Reference Nucleoprotein (N) Structural virion protein (about 48 kD). Oligomerizes and encapsidates orthohantaviral genomic segments and hence component of RNPs. Binds to L and G N ( Mir et al., 2010 , Reuter and Krüger 2018 , Arragain et al., 2019 ) Nonstructural protein (NSs) Non-structural protein encoded by some orhtohantaviruses. Has type I interferon inhibitory activity. May occur as multiple isoforms. ( Vera-Otarola et al., 2020 , Binder et al., 2021 ) Glycoprotein (GP) Structural virion protein consisting of two subunits (G N : about 70-75 kD, G C : about 50-55 kD). Produced via proteolytic cleavage from the genome-encoded precursor GPC. Projects from virion membranes as tetrameric GP spikes composed of G N and G C heterodimers. GP mediates cell- receptor binding; as a class II fusion machine it induces virion-cell membrane fusion and, thereby cell entry. G N binds to N and RNA ( Spiropoulou et al., 2003 , Tischler et al., 2005 , Cifuentes-Muñoz et al., 2014 , Li et al., 2016 , Willensky et al., 2016 , Rissanen et al., 2017 , Zhu et al., 2018 , Bignon et al., 2019 , Sperber et al., 2019 ) Large protein (L) Structural virion protein (246 kD) with RdRP, helicase, and endoribonuclease domains. Component of the RNP inside virions. Binds to N and RNA. Oligomerizes and mediates transcription and replication of viral RNA segments. Mediates cap-snatching for viral mRNA capping. ( Kukkonen et al., 2005 , Cheng et al., 2014 , Rothenberger et al., 2016 , Jeeva et al., 2019 , Durieux Trouilleton et al., 2023 ) Genome organization and replication The orthohantavirus genome consists of three negative-sense RNA molecules, termed S (small), M (medium), and L (large) (Figure 2 Orthohantavirus ). Figure 2 Orthohantavirus. Schematic representation of orthohantavirus genome organization. Orthohantavirus genomic segments assume circular forms via non-covalent binding of complementary and highly conserved 3′- and 5′-terminal sequences. Genomic segment reassortment mostly occurs among viruses belonging to the same species but interspecies reassortment likely occurs occasionally ( Klempa 2018 , Petazzi et al., 2021 ). Orthohantavirus infection starts with virion attachment, mediated by G N and G C , to cell-surface attachment factors and entry via the endosomal route ( Shtanko et al., 2014 ) (Figure 3 Orthohantavirus ). Figure 3 Orthohantavirus . Lifecycle of orthohantaviruses. (1) Virion attachment; (2) virion uptake; (3) virion-cell membrane fusion; (4) transcription; (5) translation; (6) replication; (7) virion assembly; and (8) virion egress. After engaging a cellular receptor, protocadherin 1 in the case of American orthohantaviruses, virion-membrane fusion results in early or late endosomal release, depending on the virus, of the virion RNP complex into the cytoplasm in a pH-dependent manner ( Jangra et al., 2018 , Mittler et al., 2019 , Torriani et al., 2019 , Dieterle et al., 2021 ). During primary transcription L generates uncapped antigenomic RNA molecules that are then capped using host cell-derived capped primers (cap snatching). L and S segment-transcribed mRNAs are translated by free ribosomes ( Garcin et al., 1995 , Jeeva et al., 2019 , Meier et al., 2021 ). M segment-transcribed mRNA is translated by membrane-bound ribosomes, with the expressed GPC which is likely co-translationally cleaved by cellular proteases to yield G N and G C . The synthesis of the antigenome by L protein serves as a template for genomic RNA replication. Secondary transcription amplifies the synthesis of mRNAs and genome replication. During morphogenesis, G N and G C accumulate in the Golgi apparatus, modified host membranes are acquired, and virions bud into the Golgi. Biology Genus Orthohantavirus includes 38 species for 60 distinct viruses. These viruses and their unclassified, potential relatives, subclinically, and often chronically, infect specific soricid and talpid eulipotyphla, and muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents ( Jonsson et al., 2010 , Wu et al., 2018 , Chen et al., 2023 , Kuhn and Schmaljohn 2023 ). Orthohantaviruses are overall distributed worldwide, but the individual geographic distribution is dependent on host range. At least some orthohantaviruses may not be as restricted to a particular host as originally thought, but may be carried by animals of multiple hosts ( Goodfellow et al., 2021 , Quizon et al., 2022 , Gu et al., 2023 ). Orthohantaviruses are the only hantavirids known to cause disease (all in humans) and all disease-causing viruses are rodent-borne. Human infection may occur after inhalation of aerosolized excreta or secreta of infected rodents or direct rodent contact. The two WHO-recognized orthohantavirus-caused diseases are hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS), caused by Asian and European viruses (in particular Dobrava virus [DOBV], Hantaan virus [HTNV], Puumala virus [PUUV] and Seoul virus [SEOV]), and hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS), a frequently lethal disease caused by viruses from the Americas (in particular Andes virus [ANDV] and Sin Nombre virus [SNV]) ( Vial et al., 2023 ). The hamster model of wild-type ANDV infection is lethal and largely mimics human HPS ( Hooper et al., 2001 ). After SNV infection in nonhuman primates, some animals develop an HPS-like disease, but cell passage of SNV can result in mutations that render the virus non-pathogenic in this model ( Safronetz et al., 2014 ). Many rodent models result in a chronic, asymptomatic infection, and these have been used in early-stage therapeutic studies ( Strandin et al., 2020 ). However, recent advances have been made in development of an HFRS mouse model using HTNV variants ( Wei et al., 2022 ). Orthohantavirus animal models are reviewed in ( Golden et al., 2015 ). Antigenicity There is considerable serum cross-reactivity between the nucleocapsids of hantavirids, but neutralizing antibodies targeting Gn/Gc are more specific. SNV was first identified as a hantavirid based on cross-reactivity of patient sera with other orthohantavirus antigens ( 1993 ). Similarly, rodents that carried SNV were identified in serosurveys using non-SNV orthohantavirus antigens, since SNV had not been isolated at the time ( Childs et al., 1994 ). Species demarcation criteria Demarcation of species is based upon DivErsity pArtitioning by hieRarchical Clustering (DEmARC) analysis) using concatenated deduced S, M, and L segment expression product sequences ( Laenen et al., 2019 ). Phylogenetic relationships across the genus have been estimated using maximum likelihood trees generated from complete N, GPC and L amino-acid sequences, and for N and GPC amino-acid sequences (Figure 2 Hantaviridae ). Hantaviridae ~ ViralZone Skip to main content ViralZone Hantaviridae (taxid:1980413) VIRION Enveloped, spherical. Diameter from 80 to 120nm. GENOME Segmented Negative-stranded RNA linear genome, L segment is between 6.8 and 12 kb, M segment between 3.2 and 4.9 kb and S segment between 1 and 3 kb. Encodes for four to six proteins. Some hantaviruses also encode a NSs protein on their S segment. GENE EXPRESSION The viral RNA dependent RNA polymerase (L) binds to a promoter on each encapsidated segment, and transcribes the mRNA. These are capped by L protein during synthesis using cap snatching . Transcription is terminated by a strong hairpin sequence at the end of each gene. Puumala virus expressed an additional NSs by leaky scanning in the NS gene. ENZYMES RNA-dependent RNA polymerase L Cap Snatching endonuclease L REPLICATION CYTOPLASMIC Virus attaches to host receptors though Gn-Gc glycoprotein dimer, and is endocytosed into vesicles in the host cell. Fusion of virus membrane with the vesicle membrane; ribonucleocapsid segments are released in the cytoplasm. Transcription , viral mRNAs are capped in the cytoplasm. Replication presumably starts when enough nucleoprotein is present to encapsidate neo-synthetized antigenomes and genomes. The ribonucleocapsids buds at Golgi apparatus, releasing the virion by exocytosis. Orthohantavirus ~ ViralZone Skip to main content ViralZone Orthohantavirus (taxid:1980442) Orthohantavirus is a genus of ssRNA- viruses in the Hantaviridae family that infect Rodents and rarely humans. In humans, these viruses can cause hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome, or Hantavirus pulmonary syndromes. VIRION Enveloped, spherical. Diameter from 80 to 120nm. GENOME Segmented Negative-stranded RNA linear genome, L segment is between 6.8 and 12 kb, M segment between 3.2 and 4.9 kb and S segment between 1 and 3 kb. Encodes for four to six proteins. Some hantaviruses also encode a NSs protein on their S segment. GENE EXPRESSION The viral RNA dependent RNA polymerase (L) binds to a promoter on each encapsidated segment, and transcribes the mRNA. These are capped by L protein during synthesis using cap snatching . Transcription is terminated by a strong hairpin sequence at the end of each gene. mRNAs are capped by L protein during synthesis. Puumala virus expressed an additional NSs by leaky scanning in the NS gene. ENZYMES RNA-dependent RNA polymerase L Cap Snatching endonuclease L REPLICATION CYTOPLASMIC Virus attaches to host receptors though Gn-Gc glycoprotein dimer, and is endocytosed into vesicles in the host cell. Fusion of virus membrane with the vesicle membrane; ribonucleocapsid segments are released in the cytoplasm. Transcription , viral mRNAs are capped in the cytoplasm. Replication presumably starts when enough nucleoprotein is present to encapsidate neo-synthetized antigenomes and genomes. The ribonucleocapsids buds at Golgi apparatus, releasing the virion by exocytosis. The hantaviruses (Hantaviridae) ~ ViralZone Skip to main content ViralZone Back to Andes virus resource There are approximately 92 known viruses in the family Hantaviridae, classified into 52 species. Each species is typically adapted to a specific reservoir host, most commonly rodents, but also bats and moles. With the notable exception of Andes virus, hantaviruses are generally not transmitted from person to person. Based on their geographic distribution, evolutionary relationships, and the diseases they cause in humans, hantaviruses can be broadly divided into three major groups: New World hantaviruses (the Americas) These viruses cause Hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS), a severe respiratory disease. Important examples include Sin Nombre virus and Andes virus. Annual incidence are about 100 cases per year (Andes virus) about 25 cases per year (Sin Nombre virus) Old World hantaviruses (Asia) These viruses are responsible for Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS), which is characterized by fever, hemorrhage, and acute kidney injury. Major examples include Hantaan virus and Seoul virus. Annual incidence: more than 10,000 cases worldwide Old World hantaviruses (Europe) European hantaviruses typically cause a milder form of HFRS known as Nephropathia epidemica (NE). The main causative agents are Puumala virus and Dobrava-Belgrade virus. Annual incidence: more than 1,000 cases Together, these viruses represent an important and diverse group of zoonotic pathogens that can cause severe disease following accidental transmission from their natural animal hosts to humans.

papers.title: Hantavirus preparedness among undergraduate nursing students in the United Arab Emirates: a cross-sectional study of knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice.
papers.abstract: BACKGROUND: Hantavirus infection is uncommon in many settings but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Nursing students require disease-specific preparedness for exposure-history taking, safe prevention counselling and infection prevention during community and clinical learning. This study examined undergraduate nursing students' knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice toward hantavirus and identified associated factors. METHODS: A descriptive analytical cross-sectional survey was conducted among 236 undergraduate nursing students in one nursing program in Ras Al Khaimah, the United Arab Emirates during the 2025-2026 academic year. Data was collected using the researcher-developed Hantavirus Knowledge, Attitudes and Preventive Practices Questionnaire for Nursing Students (HKAPP-NS). Descriptive statistics, Cronbach's alpha, Pearson correlations and multivariable linear regression with HC3 robust standard errors were used. RESULTS: Knowledge was low among the 236 complete-case respondents (mean 8.13/20, SD 5.84), with 59.0% classified as having poor knowledge. Attitude was moderate (mean 33.34/50, SD 7.78), and overall practice was higher (mean 42.98/64, SD 15.83). Internal consistency was strong across domains (alpha 0.886-0.967). Attitude correlated with overall practice (r = 0.569, p < 0.001), whereas knowledge did not (r = 0.101, p = 0.120). Previous hantavirus teaching, infection prevention and control training and environmental exposure predicted higher knowledge. Attitude was the strongest independent predictor of overall practice. CONCLUSIONS: Students reported comparatively frequent precautionary practice but had important disease-specific knowledge gaps, particularly regarding safe rodent-contamination cleanup, routine vaccine availability, specific antiviral treatment and person-to-person transmission. The finding that attitude, rather than knowledge, independently predicted practice suggests that scenario-based One Health education should combine factual teaching with risk appraisal, self-efficacy building, exposure-history taking and prevention counselling to strengthen future nurses' preparedness for rodent-borne zoonotic infections.

source_records.abstract: Hantavirus infection is uncommon in many settings but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. papers.full_text: Hantavirus preparedness among undergraduate nursing students in the United Arab Emirates: a cross-sectional study of knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice Background Hantavirus infection is uncommon in many settings but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Nursing students require disease-specific preparedness for exposure-history taking, safe prevention counselling and infection prevention during community and clinical learning. This study examined undergraduate nursing students’ knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice toward hantavirus and identified associated factors. Methods A descriptive analytical cross-sectional survey was conducted among 236 undergraduate nursing students in one nursing program in Ras Al Khaimah, the United Arab Emirates during the 2025–2026 academic year. Data was collected using the researcher-developed Hantavirus Knowledge, Attitudes and Preventive Practices Questionnaire for Nursing Students (HKAPP-NS). Descriptive statistics, Cronbach’s alpha, Pearson correlations and multivariable linear regression with HC3 robust standard errors were used. Results Knowledge was low among the 236 complete-case respondents (mean 8.13/20, SD 5.84), with 59.0% classified as having poor knowledge. Attitude was moderate (mean 33.34/50, SD 7.78), and overall practice was higher (mean 42.98/64, SD 15.83). Internal consistency was strong across domains (alpha 0.886–0.967). Attitude correlated with overall practice (r = 0.569, p < 0.001), whereas knowledge did not (r = 0.101, p = 0.120). The finding that attitude, rather than knowledge, independently predicted practice suggests that scenario-based One Health education should combine factual teaching with risk appraisal, self-efficacy building, exposure-history taking and prevention counselling to strengthen future nurses’ preparedness for rodent-borne zoonotic infections. Background Hantaviruses are zoonotic viruses maintained in rodent reservoirs and are clinically important because infection, although uncommon in many settings, can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Human infection most often follows inhalation of aerosolised particles from infected rodent urine, droppings, saliva or nesting material, and exposure can also occur when contaminated material contacts broken skin or mucous membranes. Hantavirus ecology, epidemiology and clinical presentations vary globally, with manifestations including hantavirus pulmonary syndrome and haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome. Recent international attention to Andes virus has reinforced the need for health professionals to understand both the usual rodent-borne pathway and the rare person-to-person exception. In May 2026, WHO and CDC reported a multi-country Andes-virus-associated hantavirus cluster linked to cruise ship travel. Andes virus is the only hantavirus currently known to spread from person to person, usually through close contact with a symptomatic person, whereas most hantaviruses are not commonly transmitted between people. This distinction has direct implications for exposure-history taking, risk communication, triage, isolation decisions and infection prevention education. Prevention is largely behavioural and environmental. Current guidance emphasises reducing contact between people and rodents, sealing rodent entry points, storing food securely, avoiding dry sweeping or vacuuming of rodent-contaminated materials, dampening contaminated areas before cleaning and applying appropriate infection prevention and control (IPC) measures in health-care settings. Therefore, preparedness depends not only on generic IPC knowledge but also on disease-specific understanding of rodent reservoirs, exposure routes, safe cleanup and practical risk-reduction messages. Undergraduate nursing students are a relevant population for hantavirus preparedness. During community health learning, home visits, environmental health activities, primary care experiences and clinical placements, students may encounter patients with non-specific febrile illness, respiratory symptoms, household rodent contamination or environmental exposure histories. They therefore need sufficient understanding to ask about rodent exposure, recognise when escalation is needed, provide safe prevention advice and practise IPC under supervision. These competencies require a One Health approach rather than a purely biomedical or classroom-based IPC approach, because safe nursing responses to hantavirus depend on recognising connections between human symptoms, rodent reservoirs, household and environmental contamination, cleaning practices and community risk communication. Embedding One Health in nursing curricula can help students translate reservoir ecology and environmental exposure information into exposure-history taking, patient education, escalation decisions and IPC behaviours at the point of care. Knowledge-attitude-practice surveys can identify modifiable gaps in factual knowledge, risk perception and self-reported behaviours, although results should be interpreted with awareness of contextual and social desirability limitations. Evidence from nursing education research suggests that knowledge and attitudes can shape students’ infectious disease management behaviours. However, disease-specific evidence on nursing students’ hantavirus preparedness remains limited. This gap matters because general IPC instruction may not correct practical misconceptions about safe rodent-contamination cleanup, vaccine availability, specific antiviral treatment or overgeneralised assumptions about person-to-person transmission. The aim of this study was to examine undergraduate nursing students’ knowledge, attitudes/risk perception, clinical practices and preventive practices toward hantavirus and to identify associated factors. The objectives were to describe students’ sociodemographic and exposure characteristics; estimate levels of knowledge, attitude and practice; assess internal consistency of questionnaire domains; examine correlations among knowledge, attitude and practice; and identify independent predictors of knowledge, attitude and overall practice. Methods Study design and reporting This was a descriptive analytical cross-sectional study. The manuscript was prepared in accordance with the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) recommendations for cross-sectional studies. Setting, participants and recruitment The study was conducted among undergraduate nursing students enrolled in a nursing program at RAK Medical and Health Sciences University, Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates during the 2025–2026 academic year after ethical approval had been obtained. Eligible participants were undergraduate nursing students who provided informed consent and completed the structured questionnaire. Students who declined participation or submitted questionnaires with no valid domain responses were excluded from the study. A non-probability convenience sampling strategy was used. All eligible undergraduate nursing students enrolled in the program were approached through program-level recruitment procedures and invited to participate voluntarily. Recruitment was conducted across all years of study to maximize coverage of the student cohort. A total of 500 eligible students were invited, and 236 submitted questionnaires with valid demographic, exposure and domain-score data and were included in the complete-case analyses. Complete data were therefore available for 47.2% of the invited cohort. The recruitment records captured the number invited and the number with analyzable complete data, but did not permit reliable separation of nonresponse, refusal and partial completion among the remaining 264 invited students. Where a submitted questionnaire lacked information required to calculate demographic, exposure or one or more domain scores, it was excluded from the complete-case analysis. No item-level imputation was performed. Study size Study-size adequacy was assessed for multivariable regression analyses because identifying predictors of knowledge, attitude and practice was a main analytic objective. Using G*Power 3.1 for linear multiple regression (fixed model, R2 deviation from zero), with a medium effect size (f2 = 0.15), alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80 and 9 predictors in the largest model, the minimum required sample was 114 students. Allowing for 20% incomplete data gave a minimum target of 143 students. The achieved complete-case sample of 236 exceeded this requirement and Green’s regression rule of thumb (N > = 50 + 8 m; with 9 predictors, minimum N = 122). Instrument and variables Data were collected using the Hantavirus Knowledge, Attitudes and Preventive Practices Questionnaire for Nursing Students (HKAPP-NS). The HKAPP-NS, including the questionnaire items and scoring scales, was developed by the study researchers in English specifically for this study and has not been published elsewhere. Item content was informed by current WHO and CDC hantavirus guidance and KAP survey methodology. The investigator team internally reviewed draft items for clarity, relevance and consistency with current guidance and undergraduate nursing competencies. No formal face-validity or content-validity assessment by an independent expert panel was undertaken before deployment, and neither item-level nor scale-level Content Validity Indexing was calculated. Accordingly, the HKAPP-NS should be interpreted as a researcher-developed, study-specific instrument with preliminary internal-consistency evidence, rather than as a fully validated scale. The questionnaire included sociodemographic and exposure variables, a 20-item knowledge section, a 10-item attitude/risk-perception section, an 8-item clinical-practice section and an 8-item preventive-practice section. Sociodemographic and exposure variables included age, sex, year of study, residence, living arrangement, previous zoonotic disease teaching, previous hantavirus teaching, IPC clinical-placement training, reported rodent or rodent-dropping exposure and environmental exposure activities. Scoring and data management Knowledge responses were scored 1 for correct and 0 for incorrect or ‘do not know’. The correct answer was true for items 1, 2, 4–14 and 18–20, and false for items 3, 15, 16 and 17. Knowledge was categorised as poor (0–9), moderate (10–15) or good (16–20), corresponding to < 50%, 50–75% and > 75% of the total score. Attitude items used a 1–5 Likert scale; the anxiety item (item 8) was reverse scored so that higher scores reflected more favorable attitudes/risk perception. Clinical-practice and preventive-practice items used a 0–4 frequency scale, with higher scores indicating more frequent recommended practice. The overall practice score was calculated by summing clinical and preventive practice scores. Overall practice was categorized as low (0–31), moderate (32–47) or high (48–64), corresponding to < 50%, 50–75% and > 75% of the total score. For regression modelling, rodent/droppings exposure was analyzed as a dichotomous indicator of any report of seeing rodents or droppings in the past 12 months, and environmental exposure represented any listed exposure activity. Complete cases were defined as respondents with valid demographic, exposure and domain responses sufficient to calculate all study scores. Because recruitment records did not retain separate disposition codes for nonresponse, refusal and partial completion, the 264 invited students who did not contribute complete data could not be disaggregated further. Demographic, exposure, domain-score, correlation and regression analyses used the 236 complete cases; no item-level imputation was performed. Sex and gender considerations Sex was self-reported using questionnaire response categories. The study did not collect gender identity data. Because the study was not powered for sex-stratified analyses and the male subgroup was smaller than the female subgroup, sex was used as an adjustment variable rather than as the basis for subgroup interpretation. Statistical analysis Analyses were conducted using SPSS 27. Continuous variables were summarized using mean, standard deviation (SD), median, interquartile range (IQR) and range. Categorical variables were summarized using frequencies and percentages. Internal consistency was assessed using Cronbach’s alpha; very high alpha values were interpreted as evidence of internal consistency but also as possible item overlap. Pearson correlation coefficients assessed relationships among knowledge, attitude and overall practice scores. Multivariable linear regression models with HC3 robust standard errors were fitted for knowledge, attitude and overall practice scores. Model building used an a priori, conceptually guided KAP framework; no automated stepwise selection was used. The largest knowledge model included age, sex, year of study, urban residence, previous zoonotic disease teaching, previous hantavirus teaching, IPC training, rodent/droppings exposure and any environmental exposure. The attitude model was specified to test demographic, education/exposure and cognitive factors linked to risk perception: sex, previous zoonotic disease teaching, any environmental exposure and knowledge score. The overall-practice model was specified to test the environmental cue and KAP-domain predictors most directly linked to behavior: rodent/droppings exposure, attitude score and knowledge score. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05. Regression coefficients are unstandardized beta coefficients. Use of AI-assisted tools in manuscript preparation During manuscript preparation, the authors used OpenAI ChatGPT to support language editing, journal formatting and organization of the submission package. The tool was not used to generate study data or conduct statistical analyses. The authors reviewed and edited the content and take full responsibility for the submitted manuscript. Ethical considerations Ethical approval was obtained from the Human Ethics Committee, RAK Medical and Health Sciences University (RAKMHSU-HEC; approval number RAKMHSU/RES/HEC/2025-26/114; dated 2 April 2026). All procedures involving human participants were performed in accordance with relevant institutional, national and international guidelines and regulations, including the Declaration of Helsinki. Participants provided informed consent before completing the questionnaire. Participation was voluntary, and responses were analyzed anonymously or confidentially according to institutional requirements. Results Participant characteristics Of the 236 complete-case respondents, the mean age was 21.45 years (SD 2.19; range 17–30). Most respondents were female, lived in urban areas and lived in family homes. Most had not received previous zoonotic disease teaching or previous hantavirus teaching, and a minority reported completing IPC clinical-placement training. A subset reported seeing rodents or rodent droppings in the past 12 months or participating in environmental exposure activities such as cleaning dusty closed storage, farm or field visits, camping or hiking, or contact with pet rodents (Table 1). Sociodemographic and exposure characteristics of complete-case respondents (n = 236) Characteristic Category n (%) or mean (SD) Age, years Mean (SD); range 21.45 (2.19); 17–30 Sex Female 193 (81.8) Male 43 (18.2) Year of study 1st year 47 (19.9) 2nd year 36 (15.3) 3rd year 67 (28.4) 4th year 86 (36.4) Place of residence Urban 177 (75.0) Semi-urban 43 (18.2) Rural 16 (6.8) Living arrangement Family home 224 (94.9) Hostel 6 (2.5) Shared apartment 6 (2.5) Previous zoonotic disease teaching No 182 (77.1) Yes 54 (22.9) Previous hantavirus teaching No 161 (68.2) Yes 75 (31.8) IPC clinical-placement training No 200 (84.7) Yes 36 (15.3) Rodent/droppings seen in past 12 months Never 154 (65.3) Once 49 (20.8) Occasionally 30 (12.7) Frequently 3 (1.3) Exposure in past 12 months None 117 (49.6) Cleaning dusty closed storage 49 (20.8) Farm/field visits 27 (11.4) Camping/hiking 28 (11.9) Contact with pet rodents 15 (6.4) Note. IPC, infection prevention and control. Percentages may not total 100 because of rounding. Domain-score, correlation and regression analyses used 236 complete cases Domain scores and reliability Knowledge was low overall among the 236 complete-case respondents. The mean knowledge score was 8.13 out of 20 (SD 5.84); 59.0% were categorized as having poor knowledge, 19.2% had moderate knowledge and 21.8% had good knowledge. Attitude was moderate overall (mean 33.34 out of 50, SD 7.78). Overall practice had a mean of 42.98 out of 64 (SD 15.83); 23.5% of students had low practice, 32.0% had moderate practice and 44.5% had high practice. Internal consistency was strong for all domains (Table 2). full_text_sections.full_text: Hantavirus preparedness among undergraduate nursing students in the United Arab Emirates: a cross-sectional study of knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice Background Hantavirus infection is uncommon in many settings but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Nursing students require disease-specific preparedness for exposure-history taking, safe prevention counselling and infection prevention during community and clinical learning. This study examined undergraduate nursing students’ knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice toward hantavirus and identified associated factors. Methods A descriptive analytical cross-sectional survey was conducted among 236 undergraduate nursing students in one nursing program in Ras Al Khaimah, the United Arab Emirates during the 2025–2026 academic year. Data was collected using the researcher-developed Hantavirus Knowledge, Attitudes and Preventive Practices Questionnaire for Nursing Students (HKAPP-NS). Descriptive statistics, Cronbach’s alpha, Pearson correlations and multivariable linear regression with HC3 robust standard errors were used. Results Knowledge was low among the 236 complete-case respondents (mean 8.13/20, SD 5.84), with 59.0% classified as having poor knowledge. Attitude was moderate (mean 33.34/50, SD 7.78), and overall practice was higher (mean 42.98/64, SD 15.83). Internal consistency was strong across domains (alpha 0.886–0.967). Attitude correlated with overall practice (r = 0.569, p < 0.001), whereas knowledge did not (r = 0.101, p = 0.120). Previous hantavirus teaching, infection prevention and control training and environmental exposure predicted higher knowledge. Attitude was the strongest independent predictor of overall practice. Conclusions Students reported comparatively frequent precautionary practice but had important disease-specific knowledge gaps, particularly regarding safe rodent-contamination cleanup, routine vaccine availability, specific antiviral treatment and person-to-person transmission. The finding that attitude, rather than knowledge, independently predicted practice suggests that scenario-based One Health education should combine factual teaching with risk appraisal, self-efficacy building, exposure-history taking and prevention counselling to strengthen future nurses’ preparedness for rodent-borne zoonotic infections. Background Hantaviruses are zoonotic viruses maintained in rodent reservoirs and are clinically important because infection, although uncommon in many settings, can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Human infection most often follows inhalation of aerosolised particles from infected rodent urine, droppings, saliva or nesting material, and exposure can also occur when contaminated material contacts broken skin or mucous membranes. Hantavirus ecology, epidemiology and clinical presentations vary globally, with manifestations including hantavirus pulmonary syndrome and haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome. Recent international attention to Andes virus has reinforced the need for health professionals to understand both the usual rodent-borne pathway and the rare person-to-person exception. In May 2026, WHO and CDC reported a multi-country Andes-virus-associated hantavirus cluster linked to cruise ship travel. Andes virus is the only hantavirus currently known to spread from person to person, usually through close contact with a symptomatic person, whereas most hantaviruses are not commonly transmitted between people. This distinction has direct implications for exposure-history taking, risk communication, triage, isolation decisions and infection prevention education. Prevention is largely behavioural and environmental. Current guidance emphasises reducing contact between people and rodents, sealing rodent entry points, storing food securely, avoiding dry sweeping or vacuuming of rodent-contaminated materials, dampening contaminated areas before cleaning and applying appropriate infection prevention and control (IPC) measures in health-care settings. Therefore, preparedness depends not only on generic IPC knowledge but also on disease-specific understanding of rodent reservoirs, exposure routes, safe cleanup and practical risk-reduction messages. Undergraduate nursing students are a relevant population for hantavirus preparedness. During community health learning, home visits, environmental health activities, primary care experiences and clinical placements, students may encounter patients with non-specific febrile illness, respiratory symptoms, household rodent contamination or environmental exposure histories. They therefore need sufficient understanding to ask about rodent exposure, recognise when escalation is needed, provide safe prevention advice and practise IPC under supervision. These competencies require a One Health approach rather than a purely biomedical or classroom-based IPC approach, because safe nursing responses to hantavirus depend on recognising connections between human symptoms, rodent reservoirs, household and environmental contamination, cleaning practices and community risk communication. Embedding One Health in nursing curricula can help students translate reservoir ecology and environmental exposure information into exposure-history taking, patient education, escalation decisions and IPC behaviours at the point of care. Knowledge-attitude-practice surveys can identify modifiable gaps in factual knowledge, risk perception and self-reported behaviours, although results should be interpreted with awareness of contextual and social desirability limitations. Evidence from nursing education research suggests that knowledge and attitudes can shape students’ infectious disease management behaviours. However, disease-specific evidence on nursing students’ hantavirus preparedness remains limited. This gap matters because general IPC instruction may not correct practical misconceptions about safe rodent-contamination cleanup, vaccine availability, specific antiviral treatment or overgeneralised assumptions about person-to-person transmission. The aim of this study was to examine undergraduate nursing students’ knowledge, attitudes/risk perception, clinical practices and preventive practices toward hantavirus and to identify associated factors. The objectives were to describe students’ sociodemographic and exposure characteristics; estimate levels of knowledge, attitude and practice; assess internal consistency of questionnaire domains; examine correlations among knowledge, attitude and practice; and identify independent predictors of knowledge, attitude and overall practice. Methods Study design and reporting This was a descriptive analytical cross-sectional study. The manuscript was prepared in accordance with the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) recommendations for cross-sectional studies. Setting, participants and recruitment The study was conducted among undergraduate nursing students enrolled in a nursing program at RAK Medical and Health Sciences University, Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates during the 2025–2026 academic year after ethical approval had been obtained. Eligible participants were undergraduate nursing students who provided informed consent and completed the structured questionnaire. Students who declined participation or submitted questionnaires with no valid domain responses were excluded from the study. A non-probability convenience sampling strategy was used. All eligible undergraduate nursing students enrolled in the program were approached through program-level recruitment procedures and invited to participate voluntarily. Recruitment was conducted across all years of study to maximize coverage of the student cohort. A total of 500 eligible students were invited, and 236 submitted questionnaires with valid demographic, exposure and domain-score data and were included in the complete-case analyses. Complete data were therefore availab…

analysis.background: Hantavirus infection is uncommon in many settings but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease, making preparedness among health professionals important.
analysis.methods: A descriptive analytical cross‑sectional survey was conducted among 236 undergraduate nursing students in one program in Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates during the 2025‑2026 academic year; data were collected with the researcher‑developed Hantavirus Knowledge, Attitudes and Preventive Practices Questionnaire for Nursing Students (HKAPP‑NS); descriptive statistics, Cronbach's alpha, Pearson correlations and multivariable linear regression with HC3 robust standard errors were applied.
analysis.results: Mean knowledge was low (8.13/20 ± 5.84) with 59% classified as poor; attitude averaged 33.34/50 ± 7.78 and practice 42.98/64 ± 15.83; internal consistency was strong (alpha 0.886‑0.967); attitude correlated with practice (r = 0.569, p < 0.001) while knowledge did not (r = 0.101, p = 0.120); prior teaching, IPC training and environmental exposure predicted higher knowledge, and attitude was the strongest independent predictor of practice.
analysis.contribution: The study highlights specific knowledge gaps and shows that attitude, rather than knowledge, drives preventive practice, informing One Health‑based educational interventions to improve hantavirus preparedness in nursing curricula.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus infection is uncommon but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease, requiring nurse preparedness. Methods: A cross‑sectional survey of 236 UAE nursing students used a custom HKAPP‑NS questionnaire and applied descriptive, reliability, correlation and regression analyses. Results: Knowledge was low (mean 8.13/20, 59% poor); attitude moderate (33.34/50) and practice higher (42.98/64); internal consistency alpha 0.886‑0.967; attitude correlated with practice (r=0.569, p<0.001) while knowledge did not; prior teaching and IPC training predicted knowledge; attitude predicted practice. Contribution: Identifies knowledge gaps and the pivotal role of attitude, guiding One Health education to boost hantavirus preparedness. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 阿拉伯联合酋长国本科护理学生的应急准备:知识、风险感知与预防实践的横断面研究。

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒感染在许多环境中并不常见,但可导致严重的肺部或肾脏疾病,因而医护人员的应急准备十分重要。
analysis_zh.methods: 在2025‐2026学年,对阿拉伯联合酋长国拉斯海马的一个护理专业的236名本科护理学生进行了一项描述性分析性横断面调查;数据使用研究者自行编制的汉坦病毒护理学生知识、态度与预防实践问卷(HKAPP‐NS)收集;采用描述性统计、Cronbach α、Pearson相关以及带HC3稳健标准误的多变量线性回归进行分析。
analysis_zh.results: 知识均值偏低(8.13/20 ± 5.84),其中59%被归为差;态度平均为33.34/50 ± 7.78,实践为42.98/64 ± 15.83;内部一致性良好(α 0.886‐0.967);态度与实践呈正相关(r = 0.569, p < 0.001),而知识与实践无显著相关(r = 0.101, p = 0.120);此前的教学、IPC培训及环境暴露可预测更高的知识水平,且态度是实践的最强独立预测因素。
analysis_zh.contribution: 研究指出了具体的知识缺口,并表明态度而非知识驱动预防实践,为基于One Health的教育干预提供依据,以提升护理课程中的汉坦病毒应急准备水平。
analysis_zh.limitations: 在提供的证据中未报告。

papers.title: Hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in children: a systematic scoping review of presentation, critical care management, and outcomes.
papers.abstract: BACKGROUND: Pediatric hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome/hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HCPS/HPS) is rare but can progress rapidly from a nonspecific febrile prodrome to respiratory failure, shock, and multiorgan dysfunction. Pediatric-specific evidence is limited, and a comprehensive map of the clinical literature is lacking. METHODS: We conducted a systematic scoping review per Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidance (protocol registered at DOI:10.17605/OSF.IO/MZDN4). PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science, and Cochrane CENTRAL were searched from inception to May 2026. Eligible reports described children or adolescents (age ≤18 years) with HCPS/HPS and extractable pediatric clinical data. Two reviewers independently screened records, assessed overlap, extracted data, and appraised reporting quality using Joanna Briggs Institute tools. RESULTS: Of 2208 database records, 20 reports were included in the core pediatric synthesis after removal of 976 duplicates and screening. Reports were concentrated in the Americas. Recurrent features included fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, myalgia, thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, pulmonary edema, respiratory failure, and shock. Severe cases required mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation, and in some cases renal replacement therapy. Larger pediatric case series and surveillance-level reports reported mortality of approximately 33% to 37%. Evidence was predominantly case-based with variable reporting completeness. CONCLUSIONS: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but potentially rapidly fatal. Early suspicion in endemic regions or after rodent exposure, with prompt supportive critical care and timely consideration of advanced cardiopulmonary support, is central to management.

Recurrent features included fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, myalgia, thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, pulmonary edema, respiratory failure, and shock. Severe cases required mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation, and in some cases renal replacement therapy. Larger pediatric case series and surveillance-level reports reported mortality of approximately 33% to 37%. Evidence was predominantly case-based with variable reporting completeness. CONCLUSIONS: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but potentially rapidly fatal. Early suspicion in endemic regions or after rodent exposure, with prompt supportive critical care and timely consideration of advanced cardiopulmonary support, is central to management.

analysis.background: Pediatric hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome/hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HCPS/HPS) is rare but can progress rapidly from a nonspecific febrile prodrome to respiratory failure, shock, and multiorgan dysfunction.
analysis.main_directions: The review maps pediatric clinical presentations, critical‑care interventions (mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, ECMO, renal replacement) and outcomes for children ≤18 years, primarily in the Americas.
analysis.current_state: Common features include fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, myalgia, thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, pulmonary edema, respiratory failure and shock, with mortality reported at roughly 33‑37 % in larger pediatric series.
analysis.gaps: The evidence is predominantly case‑based with variable reporting completeness, preventing robust epidemiologic or therapeutic conclusions.
analysis.future_research: Prospective, multicenter surveillance with standardized reporting of pediatric HCPS/HPS cases is needed to clarify risk factors, optimal supportive care, and long‑term outcomes.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but can progress rapidly from a nonspecific febrile prodrome to respiratory failure, shock, and multiorgan dysfunction. Main directions: The review maps pediatric clinical presentations, critical‑care interventions (mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, ECMO, renal replacement) and outcomes for children ≤18 years, primarily in the Americas. Current state: Common features include fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, myalgia, thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, pulmonary edema, respiratory failure and shock, with mortality reported at roughly 33‑37 % in larger pediatric series. Gaps: The evidence is predominantly case‑based with variable reporting completeness, preventing robust epidemiologic or therapeutic conclusions. Future research: Prospective, multicenter surveillance with standardized reporting of pediatric HCPS/HPS cases is needed to clarify risk factors, optimal supportive care, and long‑term outcomes.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒心肺综合征 在儿童中的研究:关于表现、危重症管理与结局的系统性范围综述

analysis_zh.background: 儿科 汉坦病毒心肺综合征/汉坦病毒肺综合征(汉坦病毒心肺综合征/汉坦病毒肺综合征)罕见,但可从非特异性发热前驱期迅速进展至呼吸衰竭、休克和多器官功能障碍。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本综述绘制了≤18岁儿童的临床表现、重症监护干预(机械通气、血管活性药物支持、体外膜氧合、肾脏替代治疗)及结局,主要聚焦于美洲地区。
analysis_zh.gaps: 证据主要为病例报告,报告完整性差异较大,难以得出可靠的流行病学或治疗学结论。
analysis_zh.future_research: 需要前瞻性、多中心的儿科汉坦病毒心肺综合征/汉坦病毒肺综合征病例标准化报告监测,以阐明风险因素、最佳支持治疗及长期结局。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：儿科 汉坦病毒心肺综合征/汉坦病毒肺综合征(汉坦病毒心肺综合征/汉坦病毒肺综合征)罕见,但可从非特异性发热前驱期迅速进展至呼吸衰竭、休克和多器… 主要方向：本综述绘制了≤18岁儿童的临床表现、重症监护干预(机械通气、血管活性药物支持、体外膜氧合、肾脏替代治疗)及结局,主要聚焦于美洲地区。 研究现状：未报告 不足：证据主要为病例报告,报告完整性差异较大,难以得出可靠的流行病学或治疗学结论。 后续研究：需要前瞻性、多中心的儿科汉坦病毒心肺综合征/汉坦病毒肺综合征病例标准化报告监测,以阐明风险因素、最佳支持治疗及长期结局。

papers.title: Can hantaviruses cause pandemics?
papers.abstract: Abstract The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns regarding the pandemic potential of hantaviruses, particularly because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proved human-to-human transmission. These concerns and fears are justified by the profound negative impact of the recent COVID-19 pandemic. Hantaviruses are segmented negative-sense RNA viruses maintained mainly in rodents and transmitted to man primarily through inhalation of contaminated aerosols. Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome predominates in Europe and Asia, whereas hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome occurs in North and South Americas and carries high mortality. Although most hantaviruses have no human-to-human transmissibility, ANDV outbreaks demonstrate that transmission between humans is possible under close-contact conditions. Comparison with previous pandemics and WHO priority pathogens highlights several pandemic-associated features shared by hantaviruses, including zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences. In this review, we will try to find the answer for the question “Can hantaviruses cause the next pandemic?”.
papers.journal: The Egyptian Journal of Internal Medicine
